content.text: Why Flat Vector Search Misses Multi-Hop Knowledge

Standard RAG retrieves isolated text chunks. When a question requires connecting facts across multiple documents — "which models use the attention mechanism introduced in the Transformer paper, and what are their differences?" — flat retrieval either misses half the relevant chunks or returns them without their relational context. Graph RAG addresses this by representing knowledge as an explicit graph: entities (people, concepts, components) become nodes, and relationships (uses, extends, part-of, created-by) become labelled edges. Retrieval traverses the graph from query-matched nodes to their neighbours, collecting connected facts that a flat vector search would never surface together.

attrs.alt: Knowledge graph with Transformer, Attention, BERT, GPT, Positional Encoding nodes connected by typed edges; query traverses from Attention node to retrieve its subgraph
attrs.title: Graph RAG knowledge graph traversal

content.text: Microsoft GraphRAG: Community-Based Summarisation

Microsoft's GraphRAG pipeline (open-sourced in 2024) extracts entities and relationships from documents using an LLM, builds a knowledge graph, detects graph communities (clusters of densely connected entities), and generates community summaries. At query time it searches community summaries rather than raw text chunks, then performs local search for specific entity facts. This two-level architecture handles both high-level thematic queries (served by community summaries) and precise factual queries (served by entity-level retrieval).

# pip install graphrag
# Microsoft GraphRAG — full pipeline via CLI

# 1. Initialise workspace
# graphrag init --root ./graphrag_workspace

# 2. Place documents in ./graphrag_workspace/input/
# Configure settings.yml (LLM model, embedding model, etc.)

# 3. Index (extracts entities, builds graph, detects communities)
# graphrag index --root ./graphrag_workspace

# 4. Query
# graphrag query --root ./graphrag_workspace \
#   --method global \
#   --query "What are the main themes in the transformer literature?"

# Python SDK usage (post-index)
import asyncio
from graphrag.query.context_builder.entity_extraction import EntityVectorStoreKey
from graphrag.query.llm.oai.chat_openai import ChatOpenAI
from graphrag.query.llm.oai.embedding import OpenAIEmbedding
from graphrag.query.structured_search.global_search.community_context import GlobalCommunityContext
from graphrag.query.structured_search.global_search.search import GlobalSearch

# Load pre-built index artifacts (community reports, entity tables)
# and run a global search over community summaries
# (full setup requires loading parquet files from the index output)
print("GraphRAG index built — use CLI or SDK for queries")
Building a Knowledge Graph with LangChain + Neo4j

For production graph RAG without the full GraphRAG pipeline, LangChain's LLMGraphTransformer extracts entity-relationship triples from documents and stores them in a Neo4j graph database. The Neo4jVector integration then provides hybrid retrieval: vector search over node properties and Cypher traversal for multi-hop relationship queries.

# pip install langchain-neo4j langchain-openai neo4j
from langchain_neo4j import Neo4jGraph, Neo4jVector
from langchain_experimental.graph_transformers import LLMGraphTransformer
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI, OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

# Connect to Neo4j
graph = Neo4jGraph(
    url="bolt://localhost:7687",
    username="neo4j",
    password="password",
)

# Extract entity-relationship graph from documents
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)
transformer = LLMGraphTransformer(llm=llm)

docs = [Document(page_content="BERT uses the Transformer encoder architecture. "
                               "GPT uses the Transformer decoder architecture. "
                               "Both BERT and GPT rely on the attention mechanism.")]

graph_docs = transformer.convert_to_graph_documents(docs)
graph.add_graph_documents(graph_docs, baseEntityLabel=True, include_source=True)

# Hybrid retrieval: vector similarity + graph traversal
vector_index = Neo4jVector.from_existing_graph(
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    graph=graph,
    search_type="hybrid",        # vector + keyword
    node_label="Document",
    text_node_properties=["text"],
    embedding_node_property="embedding",
)

retriever = vector_index.as_retriever()
results = retriever.invoke("models built on attention mechanism")
for r in results:
    print(r.page_content[:100])
Cypher Query Generation for Graph Traversal

Graph RAG can answer multi-hop questions by generating Cypher (Neo4j's graph query language) from natural language. The GraphCypherQAChain translates a question into a Cypher query, executes it against the graph, and uses the results as context for the LLM answer. This is more precise than vector search for relationship queries but requires a well-structured graph schema.

from langchain_neo4j import GraphCypherQAChain
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0)

# Chain that generates Cypher, runs it, and answers from results
chain = GraphCypherQAChain.from_llm(
    llm=llm,
    graph=graph,
    verbose=True,          # shows generated Cypher query
    validate_cypher=True,  # validates syntax before execution
    allow_dangerous_requests=True,
)

result = chain.invoke({"query": "Which models use the attention mechanism?"})
print(result["result"])

# GraphCypherQAChain generates something like:
# MATCH (m:Model)-[:USES]->(a:Mechanism {name: 'attention mechanism'})
# RETURN m.name
# Then answers: "BERT and GPT both use the attention mechanism."

# Schema introspection — helps the LLM generate valid Cypher
print(graph.schema)
Entity Extraction Quality

The quality of the knowledge graph depends entirely on the entity extraction step. LLMGraphTransformer extracts entities and relations by prompting the LLM, which introduces noise: duplicate entity names ("attention" vs "attention mechanism" vs "self-attention"), missing relations, and hallucinated edges. Improve extraction quality by restricting the allowed entity types and relationship types to a fixed schema, and by post-processing to merge near-duplicate entity names using string similarity.
When to Use Graph RAG

Graph RAG excels when queries are inherently relational — asking about connections, hierarchies, provenance, or multi-hop facts across documents. It is overkill for flat factual Q&A where the answer lives in a single passage, and it requires significant engineering investment: LLM-based extraction, graph database operations, schema design, and entity deduplication. A hybrid approach often works best: use flat RAG for most queries and route to graph traversal only for detected multi-hop questions (identified by a fast query classifier).
Hybrid Graph + Vector RAG Chain

The most practical Graph RAG deployment combines graph traversal for structured relational context with vector retrieval for unstructured prose. A query classifier routes the question: relationship queries go to the Cypher chain, factual lookups go to the vector store, and both results are merged before the LLM generates the final answer. This architecture handles the full range of query types without requiring every question to traverse the graph.
Evaluating Graph RAG with Multi-Hop Benchmarks

Standard RAG benchmarks (RAGAS faithfulness, answer relevancy) measure single-hop factual accuracy. Graph RAG needs multi-hop evaluation metrics. The key metric is multi-hop recall: the fraction of correct intermediate entities correctly traversed on the path from query entity to answer entity. MuSiQue and HotpotQA are standard multi-hop Q&A datasets for benchmarking. A simpler proxy is path accuracy: given a known entity chain (A → B → C), does the retrieval correctly surface all three nodes?
Summary
Graph RAG represents knowledge as entity-relationship graphs, enabling multi-hop retrieval that flat vector search cannot perform.
Microsoft GraphRAG uses community detection + summary indexing for global thematic queries.
LangChain
LLMGraphTransformer
+ Neo4j provides a production-ready entity extraction and graph storage pipeline.
Constrain entity/relation types with
allowed_nodes
and
allowed_relationships
to reduce extraction noise.
Route relational queries to graph traversal and factual queries to flat RAG for the best latency/precision trade-off.
